canonical_definition: In the provided literature, formal linguistic competence is defined as the ability to generate fluent and grammatically correct text based on statistical patterns learned from training data. This competence is explicitly framed as a surface-level capability reflecting 'data' memory, rather than deep semantic understanding. As one paper states, it "mainly comes from statistical learning during training on large text corpora" (EcoLoRA: Communication-Efficient Federated Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models). To operationalize and measure this construct, the surveyed work uses specific benchmarks, namely BLiMP and SyntaxGym. Additionally, formal linguistic competence is noted to correlate with brain alignment.
papers: Analysing Chain of Thought Dynamics: Active Guidance or Unfaithful Post-hoc Rationalisation?
EcoLoRA: Communication-Efficient Federated Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models
Sketch-of-Thought: EfficientLLMReasoning with Adaptive Cognitive-Inspired Sketching

definitions.text: The ability to generate fluent and grammatically correct text based on statistical patterns in training data, reflecting surface-level 'data' memory rather than deep semantic understanding.
